memes.label: "A NEW ATTACK ON MARRIAGE"
memes.description: "Chapter: A NEW ATTACK ON MARRIAGE"

memes.label: "A PROBABLE EFFECT OF GIVING WOMEN THE VOTE"
memes.description: "Chapter: A PROBABLE EFFECT OF GIVING WOMEN THE VOTE"

memes.label: Adultery
memes.description: Shaw 将通奸解构为性别化的法律建构——女性的侵占（malversation）与男性的盗窃（theft），批判其作为离婚首要理由的性别不对等。

memes.label: America
memes.description: America as a comparative reference point in Bernard Shaw's Getting Married — American divorce laws, Mormon polygyny, and constitutional experiments as foils for English marriage law.

memes.label: "CARRYING THE WAR INTO THE ENEMY'S COUNTRY"
memes.description: "Chapter: CARRYING THE WAR INTO THE ENEMY'S COUNTRY"

memes.label: Cecil Sykes
memes.description: A young suitor in Getting Married whose position illustrates the economic pressures on marriage choices.

memes.label: Celibacy
memes.description: The religious ideal of abstention from marriage and sexual relations, which Shaw critiques as both hypocritical and as an involuntary consequence of bad marriage law.

memes.label: Charles Dickens
memes.description: Charles Dickens as a literary reference in Bernard Shaw's Getting Married — invoked as the preeminent English novelist of social reality and childhood experience.

memes.label: Chelsea
memes.description: The setting of Bernard Shaw's play *Getting Married* — the Norman kitchen of the Bishop of Chelsea's Palace, where all the play's action takes place on a spring morning in 1908.

memes.label: Children
memes.description: Shaw 笔下的子女——婚姻的"真正目的"还是被忽视的第三方？

memes.label: "CHRISTIAN MARRIAGE"
memes.description: "Chapter: CHRISTIAN MARRIAGE"

memes.label: Christianity
memes.description: The dominant religious framework of Edwardian Britain, which Shaw argues began with an attack on marriage and whose contradictory stance on sex remains a source of social dysfunction.

memes.label: Church of England
memes.description: The established Christian church of England, which Shaw critiques for its incoherent marriage doctrine — a contradictory "shandygaff" compounded from Reformation, Restoration, and Elizabethan compromises.
aliases: Articles of the Church of England

memes.label: Class
memes.description: The social stratification that determines who benefits from marriage law and who suffers — Shaw argues that class interest shapes every aspect of the marriage debate.

memes.label: "CONCLUSIONS"
memes.description: "Chapter: CONCLUSIONS"

memes.label: Contract
memes.description: Shaw's proposal to reconceive marriage as a civil contract — freely entered, freely terminable — rather than a sacramental bond or property transaction.

memes.label: Deceased Wife's Sister Act 1907
memes.description: The 1907 Act of Parliament legalizing marriage between a widower and his deceased wife's sister, which Shaw cites as an example of how timidly and reluctantly the British state approaches marriage law reform.
aliases: Marriage with Deceased Wife's Sister Act

memes.label: Democracy
memes.description: In Shaw's analysis, a system of governance that requires expert statesmanship rather than majoritarian ignorance — especially when applied to questions of sexual morals.

memes.label: "DIFFERENCE BETWEEN ORIENTAL AND OCCIDENTAL POLYGYNY"
memes.description: "Chapter: DIFFERENCE BETWEEN ORIENTAL AND OCCIDENTAL POLYGYNY"

memes.label: "DIFFICULTY OF OBTAINING EVIDENCE"
memes.description: "Chapter: DIFFICULTY OF OBTAINING EVIDENCE"

memes.label: "DIVORCE"
memes.description: "Chapter: DIVORCE"

memes.label: "DIVORCE A SACRAMENTAL DUTY"
memes.description: "Chapter: DIVORCE A SACRAMENTAL DUTY"

memes.label: Divorce Court
memes.description: The Divorce Court as an institution in Bernard Shaw's Getting Married — Shaw's critique of judicial involvement in marital dissolution and the social stigma attached to divorce proceedings.
aliases: Court for Divorce and Matrimonial Causes

memes.label: Divorce
memes.description: Shaw 笔下的离婚：不是婚姻的失败，而是婚姻不存时的道德义务

memes.label: Divorce Law Reform
memes.description: The movement for divorce law reform as context for and subject of Bernard Shaw's Getting Married (1908) — the legal framework Shaw sought to transform.

memes.label: "DIVORCE WITHOUT ASKING WHY"
memes.description: "Chapter: DIVORCE WITHOUT ASKING WHY"

memes.label: "DOMESTIC CHANGE OF AIR"
memes.description: "Chapter: DOMESTIC CHANGE OF AIR"

memes.label: Duty
memes.description: The concept of moral obligation that Shaw deploys strategically — duty to divorce a failed marriage, duty to reform bad laws, duty to protect children rather than preserve marriages.

memes.label: "ECONOMIC SLAVERY AGAIN THE ROOT DIFFICULTY"
memes.description: "Chapter: ECONOMIC SLAVERY AGAIN THE ROOT DIFFICULTY"

memes.label: Edith Bridgenorth
memes.description: The Bishops wife in Getting Married, whose wedding day crisis drives the plays action and illustrates Shaws argument about the coercive nature of conventional marriage.

memes.label: England
memes.description: The national jurisdiction whose marriage law Bernard Shaw subjects to sustained critique — an "inhuman and unreasonable" system that reflects England's peculiar religious and political compromises.

memes.label: Equality
memes.description: Shaw's invocation of equality — between men and women, rich and poor — as the standard against which marriage law must be measured.

memes.label: Europe
memes.description: Continental Europe as a comparative frame in Shaw's preface — a civilization that shares England's marriage problems but approaches them with different legal and moral traditions.

memes.label: "FOR BETTER FOR WORSE"
memes.description: "Chapter: FOR BETTER FOR WORSE"

memes.label: France
memes.description: France appears in Shaw's preface as a comparative case — a nation whose marriage and population policies Shaw uses to illuminate English failures by contrast.

memes.label: Freedom
memes.description: Shaw's conception of freedom as the capacity to enter and exit marriage voluntarily — economic independence for women is the precondition of genuine marital freedom.

memes.label: General Bridgenorth
memes.description: A retired military officer in Getting Married whose persistent proposals to Lesbia Grantham illustrate the futility of romantic pursuit within a broken marriage system.

memes.label: "GETTING MARRIED"
memes.description: "Chapter: GETTING MARRIED"

memes.label: Girolamo Savonarola
memes.description: Dominican friar and moral reformer whom Shaw invokes as the paradigmatic case of "dangerous goodness" — illustrating that immoderate virtue can be more destructive than immoderate vice.

memes.label: Government
memes.description: The role of Government and the State in regulating marriage, as analyzed in Bernard Shaw's Getting Married — Shaw's argument that the State has both abdicated its responsibilities and overreached in marriage law.

memes.label: "HEARTH AND HOME"
memes.description: "Chapter: HEARTH AND HOME"

memes.label: Henrik Ibsen
memes.description: Norwegian playwright whom Shaw references for the "chain stitch" metaphor — the fragility of marriage as an institution that unravels entirely when a single thread is pulled.

memes.label: Home
memes.description: Shaw 笔下的"家"——一个被过度神圣化的虚伪空间，家庭礼貌即坏礼貌

memes.label: "HOME MANNERS ARE BAD MANNERS"
memes.description: "Chapter: HOME MANNERS ARE BAD MANNERS"

memes.label: Horatio Nelson
memes.description: British admiral whom Shaw cites as an example of society's hypocritical double standard on marriage — publicly idolized despite openly leaving his wife for a ménage à trois, contrasted with the ostracized Shelley who merely criticized the institution.

memes.label: Henrik Ibsen
memes.description: The Norwegian playwright Henrik Ibsen (1828–1906), whose plays and chain-stitch metaphor Shaw deploys to explain why marriage reform is so politically dangerous — pulling one thread unravels the entire institution.

memes.label: "IBSEN'S CHAIN STITCH"
memes.description: "Chapter: IBSEN'S CHAIN STITCH"

memes.label: Impersonality
memes.description: Shaw's concept of treating the sexual relation as an impersonal social transaction — distinct from promiscuity and foundational to his case for marriage law reform.

memes.label: "IMPERSONALITY IS NOT PROMISCUITY"
memes.description: "Chapter: IMPERSONALITY IS NOT PROMISCUITY"

memes.label: "IMPORTANCE OF SENTIMENTAL GRIEVANCE"
memes.description: "Chapter: IMPORTANCE OF SENTIMENTAL GRIEVANCE"

memes.label: Labor Exchanges
memes.description: The state-run employment offices established by the British government under the Labour Exchanges Act 1909, which Shaw endorses as the institutional mechanism for achieving women's economic independence — the necessary precondition for genuine marriage reform.

memes.label: "LABOR EXCHANGES AND THE WHITE SLAVERY"
memes.description: "Chapter: LABOR EXCHANGES AND THE WHITE SLAVERY"

memes.label: Laodiceanism
memes.description: Shaw 借《启示录》中老底嘉教会"不冷不热"的典故，提出的反狂热主义道德态度——主张在善行与日常生活中保持节制，反对道德偏执。

memes.label: "LARGE AND SMALL FAMILIES"
memes.description: "Chapter: LARGE AND SMALL FAMILIES"

memes.label: Leo Bridgenorth
memes.description: The Bishop's son in Getting Married, whose unconventional romantic preferences challenge both family expectations and conventional marriage morality.

memes.label: Lesbia Grantham
memes.description: A central female character in Shaw's Getting Married — a woman who refuses marriage on principle, embodying Shaw's argument for female autonomy within the existing system.

memes.label: London
memes.description: London as the social and geographical setting of Bernard Shaw's Getting Married — the metropolitan context of Edwardian marriage debates and class dynamics.

memes.label: Marriage
memes.description: The social and legal institution of marriage as critically examined by Bernard Shaw in Getting Married — its laws, contradictions, and proposed reforms.

memes.label: "MARRIAGE AS A MAGIC SPELL"
memes.description: "Chapter: MARRIAGE AS A MAGIC SPELL"

memes.label: Marriage Law Reform Movement
memes.description: The late 19th and early 20th century movement to reform British marriage law, which Shaw analyzes at length in his preface and which provides the political context for the play.

memes.label: "MARRIAGE NEVERTHELESS INEVITABLE"
memes.description: "Chapter: MARRIAGE NEVERTHELESS INEVITABLE"

memes.label: John Milton
memes.description: The poet and prose writer John Milton (1608–1674), whose divorce tracts Shaw cites as the origin of a reformist tradition in English-speaking culture — the "great Miltonic tradition" that dares to discuss sex and marriage openly.

memes.label: Monogamy
memes.description: The institution of exclusive marriage between one man and one woman, whose demographic rather than moral foundation Shaw exposes in Getting Married.

memes.label: "MONOGAMY, POLYGYNY AND POLYANDRY"
memes.description: "Chapter: MONOGAMY, POLYGYNY AND POLYANDRY"

memes.label: Morality
memes.description: Shaw's critique of conventional Victorian sexual morality as a mask for economic interest, and his proposal for a utilitarian morality based on human welfare rather than religious rules.

memes.label: Motherhood
memes.description: Shaw's proposal to separate motherhood from marriage — treating it as a professional public service rather than a private marital obligation.

memes.label: Mrs Bridgenorth (Edith)
memes.description: The Bishop's wife and household manager in Getting Married — already created as edith-bridgenorth; this is a supplementary page for her matriarchal role.

memes.label: Mrs George Collins
memes.description: A working-class woman in Getting Married who serves as intermediary between classes and characters, embodying Shaws democratic conviction that wisdom about marriage comes from experience, not rank.

memes.label: Nature
memes.description: The concept of Nature and the natural as deployed and critiqued in Bernard Shaw's Getting Married — how appeals to Nature underpin traditional marriage and how Shaw subverts them.

memes.label: Nell Gwynne
memes.description: Restoration actress and mistress of Charles II, invoked by Shaw as a cultural symbol of society's inverted moral valuations — the libertine is romanticized while the decorous are despised.

memes.label: Othello
memes.description: Shakespeare's tragedy *Othello* (c. 1603), which Shaw analyzes in his preface as a case study of the proprietary instinct in marriage — the husband's sense that his wife is his property.

memes.label: "OTHELLO AND DESDEMONA"
memes.description: "Chapter: OTHELLO AND DESDEMONA"

memes.label: Paris
memes.description: Paris as the symbolic destination for divorce in Shaw's preface — a city whose name stands metonymically for the liberal divorce law that England refuses to enact.

memes.label: Parliament
memes.description: The British legislature, which Shaw portrays as constitutionally incapable of addressing marriage law reform — paralyzed by electoral calculations, conventional morality, and the gap between public opinion and public interest.

memes.label: Polyandry
memes.description: A form of polygamy in which a woman has multiple husbands; in Shaw's analysis, the logical counterpart to polygyny that remains institutionally prohibited.

memes.label: Polygamy
memes.description: The practice of having multiple spouses; in Shaw's analysis, a demographic and economic question rather than a moral one.

memes.label: Polygyny
memes.description: The practice of one man having multiple wives, which Shaw analyzes not as a moral failing but as a demographic arrangement constrained by sex ratios.

memes.label: Population
memes.description: 人口数量与婚姻制度的关联——Shaw 认为婚姻法的根本问题是人口政策问题

memes.label: "Preface to Getting Married"
memes.description: "The full preface to Getting Married by Bernard Shaw, comprising 47 sections on marriage, society, and the law."

memes.label: Property
memes.description: The economic foundation of marriage, which Shaw identifies as the real obstacle to reform — attacks on marriage are perceived as attacks on property.

memes.label: Queen Victoria
memes.description: Queen Victoria appears in Shaw's preface as the symbolic representative of conventional marriage morality, imagined in an afterlife debate with Shelley — a rhetorical device Shaw uses to expose the inadequacy of moral name-calling in serious argument.

memes.label: The Protestant Reformation
memes.description: The 16th-century Protestant Reformation, which Shaw discusses as a pivotal moment in the history of Western marriage law — a rebellion against sacerdotalism that nearly abolished marriage as a sacrament but ultimately left its legal status entangled with pre-Reformation doctrine.

memes.label: Reginald Bridgenorth
memes.description: The Bishop's brother in Getting Married — a middle-aged suitor whose romantic pursuit of Lesbia dramatizes the gap between sentimental aspiration and legal reality.

memes.label: Registry Office
memes.description: The government office where civil marriages are performed, which Shaw discusses as the site where the legal reality of marriage (a state contract) is separated from its religious ceremony — and where a deeper contradiction is revealed.

memes.label: Religion
memes.description: Shaw's critique of Christianity's influence on marriage law — borrowed Eastern ideals that conflict with Western sentiment — and his call for a new religion built from Western inspiration.

memes.label: "REMOTENESS OF THE FACTS FROM THE IDEAL"
memes.description: "Chapter: REMOTENESS OF THE FACTS FROM THE IDEAL"

memes.label: Respectability
memes.description: Shaw 将"体面"（respectability）解构为区别于 morality 的外部社会规范——一种维护阶级地位与社交准入的表演性德行，而非实质的道德品质。

memes.label: Roman Catholic Church
memes.description: The Roman Catholic Church, which Shaw treats as the archetypal example of indissoluble marriage — a system so rigid that it has driven Continental Europe toward easier civil divorce as a reaction.

memes.label: Sacrament
memes.description: Shaw 的悖论式命题——婚姻是圣礼（sacrament）这一事实恰恰使离婚成为一种义务（duty），当婚姻失去"内在与精神的恩典"时。

memes.label: Sentiment
memes.description: The emotional and romantic discourse surrounding marriage that Shaw critiques as an obstacle to rational reform — sentiment masks coercion and exploitation.

memes.label: Sex
memes.description: Shaw 笔下的性——同时是生物学事实、社会制度和道德战场

memes.label: Percy Bysshe Shelley
memes.description: The Romantic poet Percy Bysshe Shelley (1792–1822), whom Shaw uses as the paradigmatic example of the cultural punishment inflicted on those who attack marriage as an institution rather than merely violating its rules in private.

memes.label: "SHELLEY AND QUEEN VICTORIA"
memes.description: "Chapter: SHELLEY AND QUEEN VICTORIA"

memes.label: Sin
memes.description: The religious category of moral transgression that Shaw argues has been misapplied to marriage — treating lawful sexual relations as sinful while ignoring real social evils.

memes.label: Sinjon Hotchkiss
memes.description: A young lawyer in Getting Married who represents the professional class's approach to marriage — contractual, rational, yet emotionally entangled.

memes.label: Slavery
memes.description: Shaw's explosive analogy between marriage and slavery — the claim that marriage without the right of exit reduces one partner to property, making the institution a form of legalized servitude.

memes.label: "SPURIOUS NATURAL AFFECTION"
memes.description: "Chapter: SPURIOUS NATURAL AFFECTION"

memes.label: "SURVIVALS OF SEX SLAVERY"
memes.description: "Chapter: SURVIVALS OF SEX SLAVERY"

relations.label: Articles of the Church of England

memes.label: The Bible
memes.description: The Christian Bible, which Shaw engages primarily through the Tenth Commandment — the prohibition on coveting a neighbor's wife that he argues is the real (and unavowed) foundation of English marriage law.

memes.label: Alfred Bridgenorth (the Bishop)
memes.description: The Bishop of Chelsea in Getting Married — a man of the Church whose authority is challenged by the marriage crisis that engulfs his household.

memes.label: The Clairvoyant
memes.description: A minor but symbolically significant character in Getting Married who represents Shaw's interest in spiritual alternatives to institutional religion.

memes.description: "Chapter: THE COST OF DIVORCE"

memes.label: William Collins
memes.description: The greengrocer and factotum to the Bishop's household in Getting Married — a working-class observer whose plain speech serves as a foil to upper-class moralizing.

memes.label: Women
memes.description: The status, economic position, and social construction of women as analyzed in Bernard Shaw's Getting Married — from "sex slavery" to proposals for economic independence and political enfranchisement.
